intro: A simplified line of the kings and queens you'll keep meeting on this trip. Tap a monarch for their story and jump straight to the chapters where they appear.

sections.title: England
sections.sub: From the Abbey's founder to the last Tudor

sections.title: The Union & Britain
sections.sub: One monarch for both kingdoms — then the United Kingdom

monarchs.name: Edward the Confessor
monarchs.blurb: The saint-king who built the first Westminster Abbey, finished just days before he died. He left no children, and the fight over his crown brought on the fateful year 1066.

links.why: His church became the coronation stage for every monarch since

monarchs.spouse: Edith of Wessex
children: None — and the empty throne set up the fight of 1066

monarchs.name: Harold II
monarchs.blurb: The last Anglo-Saxon king. He crushed the Viking invasion at Stamford Bridge near York, then marched south and lost everything at Hastings three weeks later.

links.why: His victory near York ended the Viking Age

monarchs.spouse: Edith Swanneck, then Ealdgyth of Mercia
children: Several sons, scattered into exile after Hastings

monarchs.name: William the Conqueror
monarchs.blurb: The Norman duke who won at Hastings and stamped his rule on England in stone — starting with the White Tower, built to overawe London.

links.why: His White Tower still stands at the heart of the fortress

monarchs.spouse: Matilda of Flanders
children: Ten — including the next two kings, William Rufus and Henry I

monarchs.name: Edward I "Longshanks"
monarchs.blurb: The "Hammer of the Scots". He carried off Scotland's Stone of Destiny to Westminster, and his armies met William Wallace at Stirling Bridge — and lost.

links.why: His wars made Stirling the key to Scotland
He's the king who stole the Stone of Destiny
The Coronation Chair was built to hold his stolen Stone

monarchs.spouse: Eleanor of Castile (her memorial crosses gave Charing Cross its name), then Margaret of France
children: At least sixteen with Eleanor
Edward II, who lost Bannockburn to the Bruce

monarchs.rel: End of the Wars of the Roses
monarchs.name: Richard III
monarchs.blurb: The last king of the white rose of York, and the last English king to die in battle. His young nephews vanished inside the Tower — a mystery still argued about today.

links.why: York's white rose was his family badge
The Princes in the Tower disappeared on his watch

monarchs.spouse: Anne Neville
children: One son, Edward — died at ten, a year before his father fell at Bosworth

monarchs.rel: Beat Richard at Bosworth, married his niece
monarchs.name: Henry VII
monarchs.blurb: He ended the Wars of the Roses by winning the crown in battle and marrying Elizabeth of York — joining the red rose and the white into the Tudor rose.

links.why: His marriage ended the war of the two roses

monarchs.spouse: Elizabeth of York — the marriage that ended the Wars of the Roses
children: Arthur (died at fifteen)
Margaret, whose Scottish wedding seeded the union of the crowns

monarchs.name: Henry VIII
monarchs.blurb: Six wives, a break with Rome, and an appetite for palaces. He took Hampton Court from Cardinal Wolsey, hunted where Richmond Park now stands, and sent two of his queens to the Tower.

links.why: His grandest palace, taken from his own cardinal
Two of his six wives were executed here
The mound legend puts him here, waiting for a signal from the Tower

monarchs.spouse: Six: Catherine of Aragon, Anne Boleyn, Jane Seymour, Anne of Cleves, Catherine Howard, Catherine Parr — divorced, beheaded, died, divorced, beheaded, survived
children: Mary I
Edward VI — all three reigned

monarchs.rel: Daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn
monarchs.name: Elizabeth I
monarchs.blurb: Born at Greenwich Palace, she saw off the Spanish Armada and gave her name to a golden age. She died childless — the last Tudor — and the crown passed north to Scotland.

links.why: She shares an Abbey chapel with her rival cousin Mary
Born at Greenwich Palace, which stood on this riverside

monarchs.spouse: Never married — the Virgin Queen kept her suitors as foreign policy
children: None — the crown passed to her cousin's son

monarchs.name: Robert the Bruce
monarchs.blurb: Scotland's warrior king. His victory at Bannockburn, in sight of Stirling Castle, won back the kingdom Edward Longshanks had tried to hammer flat.

links.why: Bannockburn was fought under these castle walls

monarchs.spouse: Isabella of Mar, then Elizabeth de Burgh
children: David II
Marjorie — her son began the whole Stewart line

monarchs.rel: The Bruce's line, via the Stewarts
monarchs.name: James IV
monarchs.blurb: A true Renaissance king — he funded alchemy, and his Italian alchemist famously strapped on wings and jumped from Stirling's walls. James died leading his army at Flodden.

links.why: His court alchemist tried to fly from these walls

monarchs.spouse: Margaret Tudor, Henry VII's daughter — the marriage behind the later union

monarchs.name: James V
monarchs.blurb: He built Stirling's painted Renaissance palace, with carved heads on the ceiling and unicorn tapestries on the walls, to show Europe that Scotland's court could dazzle too.

links.why: His palace of unicorns and carved heads

monarchs.spouse: Madeleine of France (died within months), then Mary of Guise
children: born six days before he died

monarchs.name: Mary, Queen of Scots
monarchs.blurb: Queen at six days old, crowned at Stirling at nine months. Her reign at Holyrood ended in scandal and flight; her cousin Elizabeth kept her prisoner for nineteen years, then had her executed. The two rivals now share the Abbey.

links.why: Crowned here as a baby
Her palace, and her showdowns with John Knox
Buried in the Abbey by the son who inherited both crowns

monarchs.spouse: Three: a French boy-king, the murdered Lord Darnley, and Bothwell — each marriage a disaster

monarchs.rel: Son of Mary, Queen of Scots — heir to Elizabeth
monarchs.name: James VI & I
monarchs.blurb: Born in a tiny room in Edinburgh Castle, king of Scotland before his first birthday. In 1603 he inherited England too — one king, two kingdoms. He also ordered the Bible translation that bears his name, at a Hampton Court conference.

links.why: Born on the castle rock
The King James Bible began at his Hampton Court conference

monarchs.spouse: Anne of Denmark
children: Henry (the promising heir, died at eighteen)
Elizabeth, the 'Winter Queen', whose line later brought in the Hanoverians

monarchs.name: Charles I
monarchs.blurb: Britain's greatest royal art collector — and the king who lost the Civil War. He was beheaded outside the Banqueting House at Whitehall, steps from where the Horse Guards stand today.

links.why: Executed at Whitehall, beside today's Horse Guards
Collector of its finest art — and later its prisoner

monarchs.spouse: Henrietta Maria of France
children: James II
Mary — mother of the future William III

monarchs.rel: Son of Charles I — restored in 1660
monarchs.name: Charles II
monarchs.blurb: The Merry Monarch of the Restoration. Legend says he ordered the Tower's ravens protected, and when Colonel Blood was caught stealing his crown, Charles pardoned him — and nobody knows why.

links.why: The raven legend is pinned on him
He pardoned the man who stole his crown

monarchs.spouse: Catherine of Braganza
children: None with the queen (a dozen otherwise) — so the crown passed to his brother James

monarchs.name: William III & Mary II
monarchs.blurb: The double monarchs of the Glorious Revolution. They hired Christopher Wren to rebuild Hampton Court into a British Versailles — a project that got exactly half finished.

links.why: Their half-built baroque palace grafted onto Henry's Tudor one

monarchs.spouse: Each other — first cousins, both grandchildren of Charles I's line
children: None — the crown moved to Mary's sister Anne, then the Hanoverians

monarchs.name: George III
monarchs.blurb: He bought a plain brick mansion called Buckingham House as a family home for Queen Charlotte — and filled it with fifteen children. His son later blew a fortune turning it into a palace.

links.why: He bought the house that became the Palace

monarchs.spouse: Queen Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz
children: Fifteen — including two kings and the father of

monarchs.name: Victoria
monarchs.blurb: The first sovereign to actually live at Buckingham Palace, moving in three weeks after her accession at eighteen. Her golden memorial now anchors the Mall's grand parade route.

links.why: First monarch to call the Palace home
The Mall was rebuilt as her grand memorial route

monarchs.spouse: Prince Albert — she wore mourning black for the forty years after he died
children: Nine, married into thrones across the continent — 'the grandmother of Europe'

monarchs.name: George VI
monarchs.blurb: The reluctant wartime king. When bombs hit Buckingham Palace in the Blitz, he and the Queen stayed put — and worked side by side with Churchill until victory.

links.why: Stayed on as bombs fell on the Palace
His war, run from Churchill's bunker

monarchs.spouse: Elizabeth Bowes-Lyon, the Queen Mother
children: Margaret

monarchs.name: Elizabeth II
monarchs.blurb: Seventy years on the throne — the longest reign in British history. Her 1953 coronation in the Abbey was the first the whole world watched on television.

links.why: Crowned in the Abbey in 1953
Her processions made the Mall the world's stage

monarchs.spouse: Prince Philip — married 73 years, the longest royal marriage
children: Anne
Andrew
Edward

monarchs.name: Charles III
monarchs.blurb: Crowned in the same ancient chair as forty of his predecessors — with the Stone of Destiny brought south from Scotland for the day, then sent straight back.

links.why: The Coronation Chair did its job again in 2023
The Stone travelled south for his coronation, then home

monarchs.spouse: Diana, Princess of Wales, then Queen Camilla
children: William, next in line
Harry
